data.title: CHI: Premium Moderates For This Convertible Fund
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (NASDAQ:CHI) has been a fund I've owned in the past, but I sold out of the position in early 2023 when the fund's premium started to get into the double digits. We also last wrote on this fund in early 2024, and at that time, the premium did decline somewhat. However, the fund has continued to see its premium come down since then, and it looks like a more interesting consideration at this time. While waiting for a discount would provide an even better bargain, one considering convertible and high-yield exposure could consider an initial entry.

CHI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.50.
  • Discount/Premium: 1.09%.
  • Distribution Yield: 11.19%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.36%.
  • Leverage: 34.32%.
  • Managed Assets: $1.236 billion.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

CHI states its objective as "seeks total return through capital appreciation and current income by investing in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities and high yield corporate bonds." This is fairly straightforward, but it can also be beneficial to retail investors who wouldn't normally have as easy access to convertible securities. Some are available, but most are only accessible to qualified institutional buyers.

The fund is leveraged, and rather highly leveraged at that. Generally, it isn't too much of an issue thanks to the fixed-income exposure of the fund. Still, this increases risks, and also the fund's total expense ratio climbs to 4.20%.

The fund is leveraged through mandatory redeemable preferred shares and a liquidity agreement. The dividend rate on these preferreds is quite low, but they unfortunately come with a redemption date in the next year or two, at a time when the replacements are having to issue higher dividend rates. For example, Series G is the latest, issued in the fall of 2024, and offers a 6.24% yield.

For the liquidity agreement, or "notes payable," the rate is based on a floating rate of OBFR plus 0.52%. As of the latest semi-annual report, the cost comes in at an average of 4.99%. When the Fed cut 100 basis points, that helped to ease the costs on this form of borrowing—and further potential rate cuts would also help.

Performance - Premium Moderates

Since our last update, the fund's total returns have been relatively mediocre when compared to the S&P 500 Index.

With convertible exposure, comparing to an equity benchmark can be more appropriate due to the convertibles' hybrid equity and fixed-income approach. That said, the S&P 500 Index during this time was mostly driven by the mega-cap names in tech, mostly the Magnificent 7. Without significant exposure to those names, we should expect to see more modest total returns on a relative basis.

When comparing the fund to the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), a non-leveraged ETF barometer for the convertible investment space, we see that CHI underperformed. That was on a total NAV return basis, which is the portfolio's underlying performance, but that was to a lesser degree. Instead, we see the most drastic difference on a total share price return basis.

That's where the closed-end fund discount/premium comes into the picture. As CHI's premium has deflated, starting mostly at the start of this year, we've seen a rather material divergence. However, that is also what makes it a more interesting choice at this time.

Looking back over the longer-term history, we still aren't seeing the fund at a bargain. Instead, it is trading relatively close to its longer-term average level. That level was helped to be pushed higher by the rather persistent premium over the last several years.

Additionally, the -1.45 1-year z-score suggests that the fund is relatively undervalued on a shorter-term basis.

Distribution - High Rate Paid Monthly

At this time, the fund's distribution rate comes to 11.19%. Given the fund is trading right around its NAV, the underlying NAV distribution yield comes in similarly at 11.30%. Calamos tends to keep its payouts at level distributions for lengthy periods of time. In other words, they don't tend to make too many adjustments, and that is often considered attractive for income investors.

Of course, that doesn't tell us whether they are earning their distribution or not. To determine that, one would have to watch NAV over time. If it is trending lower, that would mean they aren't covering the payout, and if it is flat or rising, it would suggest they are. This is because the fund will need to produce coverage primarily through capital gains in the underlying portfolio.

With a higher interest rate environment, zero-coupon convertible bonds can become less appealing. Companies will generally have to offer a bit of a coupon to make a more compelling investment argument. At the same time, companies can offer lower relative yields for convertibles due to the potential conversion feature that can make them more appealing. That can make it a more enticing option for issuing these types of securities for the company.

With that said, the fund's net investment income is still negative, meaning the entire distribution will need to be covered through gains in the underlying portfolio. That's even considering the fund carries a nearly 25% allocation to high-yield bonds.

One thing about a fund trading at a premium that is beneficial is the ability to issue shares through an at-the-market offering or the DRIP. When it is done at a premium, that is accretive to NAV, which is something CHI has been able to benefit from.

On a per-share basis, it didn't amount to too much, but at $0.02, it also isn't nothing. As the premium has come down to around parity with the NAV, this won't be a lever that will amount to anything meaningful for now.

For tax purposes, the 2024 distribution saw quite a mixture of ordinary income, return of capital, short-term capital gains, long-term capital gains, and qualified dividends. Whether this fund would be best in a taxable or tax-advantaged account will be on a case-by-case basis. However, in general, it isn't necessarily as tax-efficient as other funds available.

CHI's Portfolio

As noted, this fund is primarily exposed to convertible securities, but also has a rather material sleeve of high-yield fixed income as well. Going back to our last coverage showing the breakdown over a year and a half ago, the allocation was virtually identical. The convertible sleeve comes in at 67.44% of the fund, when it was previously 67.40%.

However, what has increased quite materially is the fund's information technology sector allocation. It had been the largest allocation of the fund, but it was at 18.9%, and consumer discretionary was the second-highest allocation at 17.3%. The divergence between the two sleeves of this allocation is quite material.

At the same time, we still don't see any Mag 7 exposure. Instead, I see primarily a lot of the newcomers to the technology space. This is to be expected because these are companies that can offer the most potential for growth or capital appreciation going forward.

That can make their convertible issuances more appealing relative to larger companies, which have mostly matured. Further, mostly below-investment-grade-rated companies issue convertibles because larger companies avoid the potential dilution and can often find other ways to raise the capital needed.

In looking at the quality allocation, we can see that the highest allocation is to unrated securities. This goes back to most of these issuances being sold to only qualified institutional buyers. Forgoing the rating process can save costs and money. It is then up to companies such as Calamos and their managers to determine their own internal ratings and whether an investment may be worthwhile.

Conclusion

CHI isn't necessarily a bargain, but the fund's premium has come down to a more modest level. That could suggest a time when investors could begin to consider potentially putting some capital to work. However, one may also consider waiting for an overall market pullback before considering investing. While CHI isn't heavily exposed to the Magnificent 7 names that have generated the bulk of the returns for the broader equity benchmarks, we tend to see broader discount widening in CEFs during more volatile times.

data.title: Metropolitan Bank: Time For A Downgrade After A Phenomenal Run
data.description:
Even the best opportunities eventually hit a point where further market-beating potential becomes limited. That, I would argue, is finally the case when it comes to Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. (NYSE:MCB). You see, back in May of this year, I reaffirmed the enterprise as a "Buy" candidate. I found myself impressed with the fact that its balance sheet was continuing to grow and that its share price was attractive. The business had successfully exited its digital payments and crypto-related business, leading to a stabilization when it came to its non-interest income and reducing overall volatility for investors. And even though asset quality was not exactly where I wanted it to be, it was attractive enough to justify maintaining it as a bullish prospect, even though I warned that a downgrade was likely if shares continued to rise.

Since that time, the stock has risen a solid 19%. That's more than double the 8.8% increase the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. When I originally wrote about the company in December 2023, I actually had rated it a "Strong Buy." Since then, the stock has risen by 91.6%. That dwarfs the 41.5% rise the broader market experienced. But now, even with asset quality at pretty solid levels, the company is finally at the point where I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is appropriate.

No longer banking on Metropolitan Bank

Perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to Metropolitan Bank would be to cover its overall balance sheet position. During the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, which is the most recent quarter for which we have data available, the bank reported deposits of $6.79 billion. That's up soundly from the $5.98 billion the company ended 2024 with. This represents a strong showing for the company at a time when many financial institutions are struggling to get deposits in the door. High interest rates have caused depositors to look elsewhere for yield. Even non-interest-bearing demand deposits, which would be the most unattractive for depositors, reported an increase from $1.33 billion at the end of last year to $1.43 billion in the second quarter of this year. But the big driver of growth ended up being money market accounts, which grew from $4.51 billion to $5.23 billion as depositors tried to capture those higher payouts.

From the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, the value of loans increased from $5.97 billion to $6.54 billion. As has historically been the case, most of the company’s exposure is to real estate. Primarily, we are talking about commercial real estate, of which the business had exposure to $4.85 billion worth at the end of the most recent quarter. By comparison, other categories are quite small. The next largest would be the commercial and industrial market, with total deposit exposure of $1.02 billion. And in a distant third place would be the $414.2 million that the company has dedicated to multifamily real estate.

Management has made sure to keep some of the cash that the company brings in dedicated to securities as well. Those amounted to $944.2 million for the second quarter of this year. That's up from the $915.8 million that the company ended last year with. Over the same period, cash and cash equivalents went from $200.3 million to $152.5 million. But this is acceptable to me when you consider that, during the same timeframe, cash and cash equivalents were slashed from $478.1 million to $238 million.

We have seen respectable growth as of late on the income statement. For the second quarter of 2025, net interest income for the company came in at $67.3 million. That's up from the $60 million the company had for the same quarter last year. The increase in the company's balance sheet certainly helped here. Another big contributor, however, was a rise in the firm's net interest margin from 3.44% to 3.83%. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that, year over year, the provision for credit losses incurred by the company grew from $1.5 million to $6.4 million. A similarly large increase for the first half of this year caused overall net interest income to climb more modestly from $119.2 million to $129.7 million.

Non-interest income, meanwhile, has been much more disappointing. In the second quarter of this year, the company generated only $2.6 million from these activities. That's down from the $6.1 million reported the previous year. The culprit here was that, last year, the business generated $3.7 million from its Global Payments Group. This year, it generated nothing from it.

This was an intentional decline, however, as management completed the company's exit from this part of the business, which operated as an issuer of debit cards for third-party debit card programs and which administered both domestic and international digital payment settlements for non-bank financial services clients, back in 2023. Its decision in 2024 to also exit the BaaS side of the business, which, like the rest of this part of the enterprise, focused on the crypto asset space, would naturally cause a decline in revenue. Despite this, the firm still managed to generate net profits in the second quarter of this year of $18.8 million. That's above the $16.8 million reported the previous year and brings results for the first half of 2025 up to $35.1 million, compared to the $33 million the company generated one year earlier.

When it comes to the banking sector, I put a lot of weight on valuation. I typically like to see institutions in this industry trade at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10 or lower. But because of how much shares of the business have risen recently, even if we project out results based on what we have seen so far for the year, we would get a price-to-earnings multiple of 11.5. Admittedly, as the chart above illustrates, this would still place the company below four of the five banks that I decided to compare it to. But still, it is more expensive than I would like it to be. In the chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, Metropolitan Bank ended up being around the middle of the group, with three of the five companies I stacked it up against being cheaper than it is. But on a price-to-tangible-book basis, only one of the companies was cheaper.

In addition to the stock being more expensive than I would like it to be, asset quality remains mixed. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 0.97%. That's just a hair below the 1% minimum that I like to see. But it also places the company around the middle of the group, as the chart illustrates. In the chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. In this case, the 10.40% reading that we get is slightly above the 10% minimum that I like to see. But once again, this places the company around the middle of the group.

Takeaway

Currently, it pains me to have to downgrade Metropolitan Bank. The bank has had a remarkable run over the last couple of years now. It certainly has been one of my better calls in this space. To be honest with you, it's not even a bad call now. But as a value investor, my objective is to find companies that are almost certain to pay off, as measured by achieving performance that is greater than the broader market for the foreseeable future. And at this point, while it is possible that Metropolitan Bank could very well continue to do just that, I don't believe that the margin of safety is sufficient to justify a "Hold" rating at this time.

data.title: Agios Pharmaceuticals: New FDA Setback Likely Temporary As Issues Can Be Mitigated
data.description:
Investment Overview

In my last post on Agios Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:AGIO) for Seeking Alpha, published mid-February, I charted the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based biotech's journey since its decision to sell its oncology business to French Pharma Servier in a $1.8bn deal, in 2017, in order to focus its attention on development of its drug candidate mitapivat, now approved (since 2022) to treat Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency, and branded as Pyrukund.

For a full history of Agios' progress, I'd refer readers to that note, but to summarize, as I wrote in February:

the company's ambition - which is inching closer to being realized - is to secure approvals for its lead drug in the indications of thalassemia and sickle cell disease ("SCD") - which are multi-billion dollar markets.

Thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder that causes your body to have less hemoglobin than normal, and can cause anemia and extreme fatigue. Agios estimates (in its 2024 annual report / 10K submission) that the disease affects 18k -23k individuals in the US, with ~6k diagnosed patients, ~70k people in Gulf Council countries ("GCC"), and potentially 1m people worldwide.

In sickle cell disease, red blood cells are misshaped, or "sickle" shaped, and can block blood flow as a result, leading to pain crises which often require medical attention or hospitalization. This is a 120k - 135k patient market in the U.S., ~150k in GCC, and >3m worldwide.

In my last note I explained that the FDA had accepted Agios' supplementary new drug application for for Pyrukund to treat adult patients with non-transfusion-dependent ("NTD") and transfusion-dependent ("TD") alpha- or beta-thalassemia, and assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act ("PDUFA") date - or decision date, in more straightforward terms, of September 7th, 2025.

Unfortunately, however, in December, Agios had shared Phase 3 data in thalassemia which revealed two patients had experienced acute liver injury, which triggered a major selloff of stock, which fell in value from >$60, to ~$25 by the end of January 2025, a loss of nearly 60%.

According to the company's 2024 annual report / 10K submission:

during the double-blind periods of ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T, two patients on PYRUKYND® experienced events of hepatocellular injury. In addition, during the open-label extension periods of both trials, a total of three patients experienced events of hepatocellular injury after switching from placebo to PYRUKYND®. All of these events occurred within the first six months of exposure to PYRUKYND® and liver tests improved following discontinuation of PYRUKYND

Agios stock had been recovering in recent months, reaching a value of >$40 per share in July, however, it is in freefall again today, as the company announced this morning that the FDA had extended its review period for mitapivat in NTD and TD by three months, to December 7th. Shares are down >10% in trading so far today, valued at $35.

Analysis - A Slight Setback Or A Major Concern? What To Read Into Delay

According to Agios' press release shared this morning:

Following a recent information request from the FDA, Agios submitted a proposed Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) to mitigate the risk of hepatocellular injury that was described in the original PYRUKYND sNDA. The submission of the REMS is a major amendment to the PYRUKYND sNDA, resulting in a three-month review extension. This extension is not the result of new or additional efficacy or safety data requested by the FDA or submitted by Agios.

Agios' CEO Brian Goff was quoted stating:

We remain confident in the favorable benefit-risk profile of PYRUKYND in thalassemia. We look forward to continuing our collaborative engagement with the FDA, with the goal of bringing this disease-modifying oral medicine to adult patients with thalassemia in the U.S.

It is not especially unusual for the FDA to delay a decision on approval of a drug, or to request additional data which is significant enough to constitute a major amendment - I have come across this on multiple occasions before in eight years covering the biotech sector. Unfortunately, I don't have any data on what percentage of companies' NDA's are subsequently approved versus denied approval, although my gut feeling is more eventually sneak over the line than are ultimately rejected.

One indication that mitapivat remains odds on for approval may be the fact that Pyrukund continues to be available in the indication of PK deficiency. If the FDA believed that the drug's side effect profile no longer supported commercial use, its likely the agency would have requested its withdrawal from the market, as was the case recently for, e.g., Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) gene therapy elevidys.

Instead, in January the FDA apparently mandated a revised safety label for Pyrukund, requiring liver function tests to be completed prior to the start of treatment, and monthly monitoring for the first six months.

This new label does not seem to have affected sales of Pyrukund, which were $12.5m in Q2, compared to $8.6m in the prior year quarter. In its Q2 earnings press release, management also shared that:

  • 248 unique patients completed prescription enrollment forms, representing an increase of 6 percent over the first quarter of 2025.
  • 142 patients are on PYRUKYND therapy, inclusive of new starts and continued therapy, representing an increase of 4 percent over the first quarter of 2025.

Still, Agios also revealed a net loss of $(112m), up from $(96.1m) in the prior year quarter, with R&D expenses of $91.9m, and SG&A expenses of $45.9m. This underlines the importance of securing approval in the much larger market of firstly NTD / TD thalassemia, followed by Sickle Cell Disease ("SCD").

There is no question that the FDA's delaying its decision will negatively affect Agios financially, to the tune of millions, perhaps tens of million of dollars. The company doubled its sales force to 40 employees last year, and not being able to execute on its launch plan until 2026 will be hugely frustrating for the management team.

Looking Ahead - Is There A Contrarian Opportunity In Play Based On A Temporary Setback?

On its Q2 earnings call with analysts, Agios' Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") Tsveta Milanova told analysts:

We are confident in our ability to deliver on the U.S. launch of PYRUKYND in thalassemia, pending FDA approval for several reasons. First, thalassemia is well diagnosed due to widespread prevalence of newborn patient screening in the U.S. In addition, given the availability of claims data and ICD-10 codes, these patients have an established record of engagement with health care services.

Second, the burden of disease is high, meaning symptomatic patients are actively managed and the associated cost of care is significant. Despite this, treatment options are limited, especially for patients with non-transfusion-dependent disease and most patients still rely on supportive therapy. Third, this is a community with a high level of engagement across key thought leaders and robust patient advocacy representation.

Lastly, robust preparedness supports our ability to deliver a successful launch. Our disease state education is tailored to address the diverse multicultural aspects of thalassemia, and we have leveraged our connections with thought leaders to provide additional disease education for community-based physicians.

I'd broadly concur with most of this, although we should note there are multiple approved options for patients with thalassemia on the market already, for example, Merck's (MRK) Reblozyl, Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Revlimid; Adakveo from Novartis (NVS); Casgevy from Vertex (VRTX)/CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP); Kuvan and Palynziq from BioMarin (BMRN); Endari from Emmaus; Besremi from PharmaEssentia; Jakafi from Incyte (INCY); and Rytelo from Geron (GERN).

We should also note, however, that mitapivat is an oral therapy, giving it a potential competitive advantage over all of the above, many of which are prescribed off-label for thalassemia, and in the case of Casgevy, while its ability to "functionally cure" patients is second to none, the treatment process can take up to a year, and an aggressive preconditioning regime can leave patients infertile.

In its pivotal study, mitapivat met its primary endpoint, increasing blood hemoglobin levels in 42.3% of patients, compared to 1.6% of patients in the placebo arm, and met key secondary endpoints also. A second pivotal study, ENERGIZE-T, also met its primary and all key secondary endpoints.

In my view, a drug with such a strong efficacy profile is unlikely to be denied approval so long as the appropriate REMS program is in place, and it is understandable that the FDA would require more information and more time to consider the implications of fresh adverse safety data, even if I am surprised the agency did not request additional information sooner.

The FDA has undergone fundamental changes owing to the change of government in the U.S., with the Trump administration replacing key figures at the agency and specifying new objectives and initiatives, hence, delays are not necessarily unexpected.

Ultimately, however, I am not sure this will derail Agios' approval application for mitapivat, which I'd still expect to be approved in December. Furthermore, the drug is likely to secure approval in Europe and the gulf regions too (where thalassemia and SCD are more prevalent), and has distribution agreements in place with Avanzanite Bioscience, in Europe, and NewBridge Pharmaceuticals FZ-LLC, in the Gulf Cooperation Council region, already established (the terms of which have not been made completely clear so far as I am aware).

Agios boasts a cash position of $1.3bn, so has the funds to initiate a major commercial launch, and when we think about the company's current $2bn market cap, and peak revenue projections of, for example, $786m by 2026, we can make a compelling case that Agios stock will surge if a delayed approval is secured in December.

While I believe the long-term future of thalassemia and SCD treatment may lie in gene therapy - Casgevy with a less onerous preconditioning regime, for example, or even in-vivo Casgevy, such developments lie many years into the future, and therefore, the evidence suggests to me that mitapivat is well-placed for standard-of-care status, and "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues before 2030.

Of course, Agios is "all in" on mitapivat - it does not have any other pipeline assets of note - therefore a full rejection in thalassemia would be disastrous for the company and its share price, and leave anybody buying the dip today with heavy losses to contemplate in three month's time.

I don't think it will play out like that, and while I don't think Agios will recapture its former share price highs of >$60 for some time, if at all, owing to the requirement for a REMS, I'd still anticipate an approval for mitapivat in thalassemia come December, which raises the prospect of a share price spike, potentially >$50, and a market cap approaching $3bn, which the company would fully merit, and which would represent a nice >35% gain for investors in a short space of time.

data.title: I Now See Chemistry Can Be Hip: I Can't Buy An Isotope, But I Can Buy ASP Isotopes Stock
data.description:
My long-time don’t-love/seriously-hate relationship with chemistry is changing.

It’s shifting toward love.

I trace my initial ambivalence to my uncle Yussie.

Back when I was around kindergarten age, he was the family genius. He was the first to attend college.

He graduated with a degree in Chemistry. And he went on to a long career in that field.

I had no clue what the heck chemistry was. So I asked him.

He explained how “everything in the world is made up of chemicals.”

I thought hard about that.

“Even bananas?” I asked.

“Yes, even bananas.”

I had to put this to a test. Even my younger self sensed something like hypothesis that should be provable.

“OK,” I answered. “Make me a banana.”

Details on what happened next remain fuzzy. But I do remember that a few moments later, he stood in front of me — and suddenly pulled a banana from behind his back.

I was crushed when I learned that my mother slyly handed it to him. I felt like chemistry was nonsense designed to fool kids — like the tooth fairy.

The New York State Board of Regents saw it differently. To graduate high school, I had to take, among other things, a year of chemistry.

That meant labs I invariably messed up — fortunately, nothing exploded. Also, I had to memorize a big chart called the Periodic Table. (Does anybody still memorize that? My class was pre-personal computing, pre-internet, pre-many things.)

I hated memorization. I still do.

I managed to barely pass the class. But I haven’t looked back to that nightmare subject — until just recently.

Centrus (LEU) came to my attention as an interesting sock. But to write it up, as I first did on 12/13/24, I had to figure out what isotopes were.

Aw, what the heck. I’m older now. And I already taught myself things like quantum computing and gene editing in order to write up such stocks on Seeking Alpha. Had I continued to resist chemistry, I might not have been able to effectively write up LEU.

So, I bit the bullet. I studied up on enrichment of uranium isotopes.

Now I see its time to raise the ante.

I came across ASP Isotopes (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:ASPI) as another uranium enrichment stock. And I discovered something long known to chemists but not chem-haters like me. There’s more much to isotopes and enrichment than just uranium. In fact…

Isotopes Are Becoming Valuable and Important Resources — and Businesses

We have to start with atoms. These are “the fundamental building blocks of matter.”

  • They have Protons, each of which is positively charged, and
  • They also have an equal number of negatively charged Electrons.

Nice balance. And it creates interesting work for electrical engineers and battery makers. But we’re not talking about electricity today.

Now, if I were the creator of the universe, I’d keep things nice and simple. The protons and electrons packed together in a nice, stable neutral (positive balancing negative) nucleus.

But the real creator had other ideas. It built the nucleus by packing together protons and neutrally charged neutrons. How many neutrons? Look that up in the periodic table! (Oh, those memories!)

  • The negatively charged electrons orbit around this nucleus packed with protons and neutrons.

OK. That was easy… especially if you’re comfortable memorizing or looking up the numbers of protons, electrons and neutrons.

But there’s more…

If I created the world, each atom would have a specific number of protons, electrons, and neutrons. But again, the genuine creator isn’t letting us off that easy.

For some elements, the number of neutrons can vary. These are called isotopes.

Here’s a broad summary from ASPI.

Here’s a hypothetical atom. I’ll call it “Gerstein” and give it the symbol “GR.” (I wanted to use GE, but a real element, Germanium, already took it!)

GR has:

  • 6 protons,
  • 6 electrons, and
  • 53 neutrons in the basic version but with some isotopes having 48 neutrons, some having 55, and some having 59.

Now, here’s where it gets interesting.

Suppose GR is not an isotope. If that were so, you could mine for a bunch of it. And all of it would be made up of atoms 6 protons, 6 electrons, and 53 neutrons.

But I hypothesized GR as an isotope. So, if you mine for a bunch of GR, nature might give you something like this:

  • 48% of what you’d mine would be basic — GR-53 (Gerstein, having 53 neutrons)
  • 37% of it would be GR-48
  • 13% of it would be GR-59, and
  • 2% of it would be GR-55

Whatever — GR still has whatever properties GR has and does the things GR does.

But, but, but…

Some scientists doing things that put listeners to sleep when they try to explain come up with an “enriched” isotope.

When they enrich, they raise the concentration of GR-55 above 2%. They might get it way up to, say, 99.75%!

Since GR doesn’t really exist, ASPI doesn’t really enrich GR-55 (also known in my imagination as Gerstein-55)

But henceforth, when we encounter things ASPI does deal with, think back to this process.

GR is not real. But the process is very real: A bunch of something taken from the ground winds up with a far higher concentration of a particular variation (isotope) than nature supplied.

The point of all this is that enriched GR-55 (or any real-life variation ASPI addresses) does something no other variation does.

A heavily concentrated uranium isotope can generate a lot of clean power. A super-duper concentrated uranium isotope can blow up the world (which is why we’re so careful about enriching uranium).

But not everything is uranium. Like other super high concentrations, heavily enriched GR-55 might be great for advanced medicine. Or it may allow semiconductor wafers to accomplish things they can’t otherwise do.

That, essentially, is what ASPI does as a business. It creates enriched isotopes that have valuable uses. ASPI plans to produce these and sell for big profits.

ASPI Is Transitioning from Being an R&D Shop to a Commercial Enterprise

Did you see the 2023 Christopher Nolan flick Oppenheimer? Or maybe you saw 1989’s Fat Man and Little Boy. Those were about the atom bomb. Perhaps you read up on LEU. (My write-ups are here and here.)

If so, you probably know that enriching isotopes is not child’s play. Even today, it’s serious business requiring serious technology.

Although ASPI is, strictly speaking, a Specialty Chemicals company, evaluate it more as you would an early development-stage biotech firm. (I call these R&D shops.) Don’t try to value ASPI as if it were like DuPont de Nemours (DD) or Celanese (CE).

ASPI is currently in the process of ramping up production capabilities and showing its efforts to prospective customers. (The aim is to have the latter "qualify" the products.)

ASPI’s Technological Processes

I wouldn’t dare try to explain the details of how ASPI does what it does. Nobody who barely passed high school chemistry should do that.

Instead, I’ll refer you directly to ASPI.

Here’s its graphic summary of the two methods it uses.

Pages 9-13 of the latest 10K explain a lot more — and they do so in reasonably clear language. There are many methods besides the two illustrated above. But those are the ones with which ASPI works.

Briefly — very, very briefly…

  • Aerodynamic Separation Process (ASP) spins gaseous material round and round to the point where the desired isotope separates from the rest. ASPI likens it to a conventional centrifuge, but with less moving parts.
  • Quantum Enrichment Technology (QE) uses lasers to stimulate and separate the desired isotopes.

Here’s another look at Quantum Enrichment.

Why ASPI’s Efforts Are Important

Again, isotope enrichment wasn’t invented just yesterday.

So is ASPI just offering a newer version of the same old things — ASPI says it can do it cheaper and/or faster. That is ASPI’s reason for being.

Companies (and consumers) today like cheaper and/or faster. So I can’t deny that as part of ASPI’s sales pitch.

But there’s a lot more to it than that.

You see, we in the west really freaked out after the 1979 accident at Pennsylvania’s Three Mile Island nuclear plant. Nuclear power became taboo for decades. So naturally, private enterprise didn’t invest much in the area.

The old Soviet Union wasn’t hampered by either obstacle. Even though it, too, experienced a 1986 nuclear-power-plant issue at Chernobyl, its command economy kept nukes going full speed ahead.

So, here’s today’s isotope supply chain.

Even aside from the mechanics of supply chain (getting things from place to place), how comfortable should anybody in the U.S., or even the West, feel about that?

Although much of ASPI’s production is in South Africa, many in the West presumably want to see it succeed.

Markets ASPI is Currently Pursuing

Presently, there are three end markets that are in ASPI’s immediate radar. These are nuclear medicine, electronics, and electric power generation.

Nuclear medicine is a step up from the sort of radiology with which we’re all familiar. The latter shows how parts of the body look. Nuclear imaging shows how they function. This happens by following a “radioactive tracer” injected into the patient’s vein or taken by mouth. (These aren’t medicinal nor are they dyes. They have no side effects.)

This emerging area is becoming big.

And ASPI has many irons in the fire, or rather, isotopes in the lab, to address this.

Moving on to electronics, it all started simply enough: Etch conductive copper material onto tiny wafers and we wind up with a semiconductor chip.

But as time passed and the industry progressed consistent with Moore’s law (the notion that “the number of transistors in an integrated circuit (IC) doubles about every two years”) put more and more pressure on manufacturers and users. There are even debates as to how long we can keep boosting chip density, and whether Moore's Law is dead.

That’s why we need, in addition to photonics, better versions of basic semiconductor materials, things that can maintain more purity, better temperature tolerances, etc. Enter isotope-based materials!

Finally, nuclear-power generation is making a comeback. I discussed this in my initial 12/18/24 LEU write-up.

LEU is a big deal because it’s the first authorized (by regulators) domestic supplier of HALEU (High-Assay Low Enriched Uranium). And it’s only at startup production levels.

HALEU is a big deal. The concentration of the most desired Uranium isotope here is much higher than is the case for what we usually use, but still far below highly dangerous levels.

ASPI is looking to get into the HALEU business.

ASPI’s current shareholders will have an interest in this through a to-be-created company. ASPI is preparing to spin this unit off to shareholders. (See below.)

Startup Progress — At a Vital Pivot Point

The company hasn’t been a startup just from an operations standpoint. Its investor communications function has also been in startup mode. I’ll refer more to that below. For now, be aware that management just issued (on 9/2/25) a substantial Shareholder Update letter. (To me, it’s the functional equivalent of major earnings call — I recommend that you read it as such.)

Early on, it delivers as much of a (positive) bang as a shareholder should want to see:

[T]he Company has brought three isotope enrichment facilities into operation.

I am delighted to report that the Company has now shipped the first samples of both Ytterbium-176 and Silicon-28 to customers, a major milestone for the Company that should allow us to fulfil indicated customer demand representing $50 to $70 million of potential revenues during 2026 and 2027 from these two isotopes alone.

In addition, the Company has started the procurement process for long lead time equipment to deliver four new laser production plants, which we plan to construct, starting in the first quarter of 2026.

After four years of hard work, the Company is now transitioning from primarily a research and development company to a commercial organization…

I’m not showing any numbers in this report. As a strictly R&D company so far, ASPI doesn't really have any numbers worth showing. But as the quoted passage states, we now have some monetary visibility on prospective change.

Going into further detail, management said:

Researching, constructing and commissioning three plants in three years is a noteworthy achievement and we are very proud of our progress.

We successfully shipped samples of both Silicon-28 and Ytterbium-176 to customers during August, in line with the guidance that we provided in July. We also expect to ship the first samples of Carbon-12 to a customer during September.

The shipping of the first enriched isotopes marks an important milestone. These last four years have been focused on research and development of new processes that will scale into a large commercial organization.

These first enrichment facilities will act as a blueprint in the future where we will implement process efficiencies learnt during the construction of these first of a kind facilities to further improve timelines and capital expenditure.

We have already commenced the procurement process of critical long lead-time components for four new laser plants, which will be used to enrich Gadolinium-160, Zinc-68, Nickel-64 and Lithium-6/7, isotopes where we have received significant customer interest.

Besides transformation to commercial activities…

ASPI Has Also Been Quite Busy on the Corporate Front

The aforementioned HALEU operation spinoff is taking shape. The new company already has a name and ticker: Quantum Leap Energy (QLE). A new CEO and a new CFO for QLE have been hired. The spinoff is likely to occur in the 2025 fourth quarter.

During 2026, ASPI, working with Fermi America, will start building plants in the U.S.

Also, a merger with South Africa based Renergen could close this quarter. When it first announced this deal on 5/25/25, ASPI said:

There will be significant end-market synergies. Liquid helium is an “irreplaceable” mineral in the medical, semiconductor and energy industries, which are also ASP Isotopes’ three target markets.

There is expected to be significant end customer overlap with the combined group servicing the semiconductor industry, medical industry, energy and space exploration. ASP Isotopes should also benefit from Renergen’s existing customer network in industrial gases significantly increasing sales targets.

It is anticipated that there will be additional substantial synergies, as much of the corporate infrastructure that ASP Isotopes expected to create during 2026 already exists at Renergen. The combined group’s headquarters will relocate to Austin, Texas, with close proximity to many of the combined group’s customers.

The combined group will help the U.S. secure supplies of critical and strategically important materials vital in many end markets which enable tomorrow’s megatrends, such as quantum computing and advanced semiconductors.

Further down in the letter, management added:

The goal of the combined group is to generate over $300 million in EBITDA in 2030, which is expected to be driven by a mix of isotopes, helium and LNG sales into the South African energy market, based on management’s current estimates, expectations and assumptions regarding the execution on ASP Isotopes’s and Renergen’s businesses strategies.

Also, as ASPI takes on more of the look and feel of the sort of public companies to which we are accustomed, the company will step up its shareholder communications efforts.

As you likely noticed, the Seeking Alpha Transcripts and Insights presentation is sparse.

But ASPI has not been silent. It’s been hosting analysts at its facilities in South Africa.

Soon, ASPI will be communicating better with the rest of us:

As part of our expansion of Investor Relations and Communications, we will now be doing quarterly online presentations and Q&A sessions to go through numbers and updates for investors. The first of these will be in November. Full access details will be released on our website nearer to the date.

Risks

The Most Basic Risk – Successful Product

As a commercial startup, ASPI bears all the usual will-it-really-work risks. Much like what I wrote about POET on 8/28/25, this is the sort of company that in olden days would have relied on venture- and/or angel-investor capital.

Today, such companies are in the public markets. Yet they present angel/venture levels of risk.

Another Basic Risk - Solvency

All such companies present solvency risk.

Seeking Alpha publishes Income Statement, Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Statement data for ASPI, as it does for all other companies. But given the current state of flux here, I don’t think it would be meaningful to show the sort of highlights I usually present.

Suffice it to say, for now, that:

  • The company had $67.7 million in Cash and Equivalents at 6/30/25, up from $61.9 million on 12/31/24.
  • Convertible Notes payable amounted to $98.1 million, up from $33.4 million.
  • Shareholders’ Equity was $135.9 million, up from $94.3 million

The latest 10-Q is available here.

Generally, as is often the case for companies like this, ASPI will raise whatever external financing it needs so long as the debt and equity markets deem its prospects credible. And that has been happening.

It’s been raising equity. It also issued convertible notes attached to QLE. And QLE’s HALEU efforts will be supported by financing from TerraPower.

Might external financings be dilutive? Be careful about oversimplifying this issue, as many do. Yes, profits are ultimately allocated among a larger number of stakeholders. But if you are unwilling to assume new funding will produce ample new sources of profit to divvy up, you should not be investing in stocks like this.

I expect ASPI to get whatever outside funding it needs until it becomes able to finance its own operations,

A Talkative Bear

Back on 11/26/24, ASPI fell 24% following a short seller report by Fuzzy Panda. I don’t typically recommend shares of companies thusly attacked. It’s often hard for outside shareholders to evaluate the merits of the competing claims.

I studied the Fuzzy Panda report as well as ASPI’s detailed rebuttal.

In my opinion, the ASPI rebuttal is as detailed and substantive as any response I’ve ever seen from management of any company subjected to a short attack. Seriously… it was devoid of self-serving fluff and long on bona fide information (to which Fuzzy Panda has not responded).

The price chart (see below) suggests the Street likewise took a good close look and came away concluding that Fuzzy Panda did not succeed in supporting its bearish case. Ditto the behavior of outside entities working with ASPI on its commercialization endeavors and supplying capital.

I’m fine with ASPI. You can judge for yourself.

A Highly Volatile Stock

Emerging tech stocks tend to be volatile. Micro-caps tend to be volatile. ASPI combines both. So, it can be very volatile.

Add to that ASPI's still nascent investor communications situation.

As I type these words (about 11am ET on 9/3/25), ASPI's Shareholder Update Letter, posted to the Investor Relations portion of its own website at 7:30am ET on 9/2/25 still has not progressed through the usual channels that typically bring such items to Seeking Alpha's company news section. (I found it only because basic research I was doing on Perplexity AI Pro cued me into the fact that the letter existed and its location.)

As noted, information flow should get a lot better starting in November. But until then, expect the current situation to add even more share-price volatility.

What to do About ASPI Stock

Not surprisingly, given that commercial activities are just starting, I can’t offer any logically sensible valuation-related numbers.

So let’s go directly to the price chart.

I wouldn’t go so far as to say it’s a mega-bull’s dream. But all things considered – ASPI’s pre-commercial stature and the late 2024 short report, I’d say the chart looks pretty darn good.

I like how the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) has, since early April, stayed above the gently rising 50-day EMA.

Look also at what Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) have been showing since April. Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Neither indicator has been bullish 100% of the time. But that’s the case for all stocks. These indicators always move up and down as investors adjust their portfolios. But both indicators have been above neutral for most of the time – more so the CMF.

The latter suggests that those institutions that had been looking at ASPI have been liking what they’ve been seeing.

With further shareholder communication scheduled to begin in November, and with the sort of financial goals management has already shared, I expect more institutions to come aboard going forward.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see ASPI as likely outperforming the broader market as word spreads about this company’s emerging commercial isotopes business.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating ASPI as a 'Buy' for those who can handle risks associated with a company that isn't merely emerging, but is EMERGING! (The good part is the now unfolding transition beyond this phase.)

data.title: 9.20% "Qualified" Return?: Good Chance With This Undervalued Enbridge Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

I have written before that the market (generally retail investors) mistakenly often looks at current yield rather than total return when selecting their investments. This leads to mispricings and undervalued securities. Our Conservative Income Portfolio services focuses on mispricings on fixed-income securities that provide a combination of current yield and price upside that should handily beat the market.

These kinds of mispricings often occur in baby bonds where investors incorrectly look at current yield rather than yield-to-maturity and yield-to-call. Mispricings can also occur in all forms of preferred stocks but reset-rate preferred stocks can be a rich source of undervalued preferred stocks. The reason being is that investors are focusing again on current yield more than what the future reset-yield will likely be which can change a reset-rate preferred stock’s attractiveness greatly. In today’s article, we will demonstrate how a reset-rate preferred stock is quite undervalued simply because its current yield is lower than other alternatives despite the fact that its total return is likely to be much better than the other alternatives.

Enbridge

Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) is an energy company with diversified assets including utilities and pipelines. It has been written about often on Seeking Alpha so I will simply summarize with Yahoo Finance’s description of Enbridge.

Enbridge Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operates as an energy infrastructure company. The company operates through four segments: Liquids Pipelines, Gas Transmission, Gas Distribution and Storage, and Renewable Power Generation. The Liquids Pipelines segment operates pipelines and related terminals to transport various grades of crude oil and other liquid hydrocarbons in Canada and the United States. This segment also provides physical commodity marketing and logistical services, and crude oil marketing services. The Gas Transmission and Midstream segment invests in natural gas pipelines and gathering and processing facilities in Canada and the United States. The Gas Distribution and Storage segment is involved in natural gas utility operations serving residential, commercial, and industrial customers in Ontario, as well as natural gas distribution activities in Quebec. The Renewable Power Generation segment operates wind, solar, geothermal, waste heat recovery, and transmission assets in North America.

Enbridge Reset-Rate Preferred Stocks

* Prices used in this article are the closing prices on August 29th

For those unfamiliar with reset-rate preferred stocks, their dividend adjusts every 5 year based on the yield of the 5 year treasury note at the time of the reset date. The first reset-date is also generally the first call date.

Enbridge has many reset-rate preferred stocks, most of them denominated in Canadian dollars. But there are 3 ENB reset rate preferred stocks that are denominated in U.S. dollars meaning that U.S. dollars are used for buying and selling these preferred stocks. In addition dividends are paid in U.S. dollars.

These 3 preferred stocks can be purchased over-the-counter at most U.S. brokerages. Canadian investors and Interactive Broker’s users will use the Toronto Exchange (TSE) symbols. Here are the OTC symbols followed by the Canadian and Interactive Broker Tickers;

EBBNF ENB.PF.U

EBBGF ENB.PR.V

EBGEF ENB.PF.V

Below is a chart of all of the important metrics for these 3 reset-rate preferred stocks.

As you can see from the “Current Yield” column, EBBNF has the lowest current yield. If you stop your analysis there, you would buy either EBBGF or EBGEF. But if you look at the “Yield To Call” (YTC) column, you can see that EBBNF has a 9.28% YTC while the other 2 are below 7.5%. And EBBNF’s “Reset Yield” is also the highest of the 3 at its current price and it resets sooner than the other 2. So is there reason to think that EBBNF could well be called? The answer is yes. And if not called, it could still be selling close to $25 giving you close to a 9.28% annualized return.

If we look at EBGEF, we see that its current yield is 6.8%. At $25, EBBNF yield would reset to 6.87%. So using EBGEF as a guide, we can see that EBBNF’s price should be around $25 on its reset date 2 years from now. EBBNF also has a better reset formula than EBGEF so that adds further to EBBNF’s relative fair value and we also see that EBGEF’s current yield is currently set to drop on its reset-date. All of this combined easily puts EBBNF’s fair value over $25 on its reset date 2 years from now.

Comparing EBBNF to EBBGF is relatively easy because they have the same reset-rate formula. Let’s say you bought the same dollar amount of EBBNF and EBBGF, let’s say $2357. That is 100 shares of EBBNF at its current price and 96.2 shares of EBBGF’s price. Doing a little math, we find that over the next 2 years EBBGF will earn 30 cents per share more in dividends than EBBNF before EBBNF resets. But the price of EBBGF is 93 cents more than EBBNF. EBBNF and EBBGF have the same exact reset-rate formula so why pay 93 cents more for EBBGF to get 30 cents more in dividends over 2 years. So EBBNF is undervalued relative to EBBGF just on that basis. But at the 2 year mark, given current interest rates, we will see EBBNF’s dividend reset higher and the current yield advantage that EBBGF currently has will disappear with EBBNF at $25. So again it looks like a $25 price is in reach for EBBNF over the next 2 years.

And remember that the dividends earned will be taxed at the “qualified dividend” tax rate and the capital gain will be taxed at the long term capital gains tax rate. So with these tax breaks, U.S. investors can expect very strong after tax returns on EBBNF.

EBBNF Versus Other BBB- Rated Preferred Stocks

EBBNF is an investment grade preferred stock with a BBB- rating from S&P. So we know that EBBNF is undervalued versus EBBGF and EBGEF but what about non-Enbridge preferred stocks with the same credit rating as EBBNF. Here is a chart of all U.S. qualified fixed-rate dividend paying preferred stocks excluding those selling over par that are callable now.

The “CY Stripped” column provides the current yield. Even EBGEF’s current yield at 6.80% is way better than your typical BBB- preferred stock which averages a yield of around 5.95%. And with EBBNF very undervalued relative to EBGEF, EBBNF looks super undervalued here. After all we are expecting something over a 9% annualized return on EBBNF over the next 2 years versus yields of around 6% for the typical BBB- rated preferred stock.

Summary/Conclusion

EBBNF is a reset-rate preferred stock that will have its dividend reset in 2 years. The low current yield has kept investors away who 1) don’t understand the stock, 2) how undervalued it is and 3) what a high return EBBNF is likely to generate.

The expected upward move in EBBNF’s dividend when it resets should propel it to a price of around $25.00 given current circumstances and when compared to where other ENB preferred stocks are currently trading. This computes to an annualized return for EBBNF of over 9% over the next 2 years. And this return will be taxed at the “qualified dividend” rate providing a very large after tax yield for U.S. taxpayers – especially for an investment grade preferred stock.

When compared to its 2 peer ENB preferred stocks, EBBNF is quite undervalued as I have shown in the article. EBBNF has a YTC of 9.28% versus YTCs of under 7.5% for the other 2 ENB preferred stocks.

And when compared with other preferred stocks with a BBB- credit rating from S&P, EBBNF is really cheap. The typical BBB- rated preferred stock is only yielding 6% right now, so EBBNF’s 9% projected return over the next 2 years looks fantastic.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AVDV Can Outperform As Interest Rates Decline
data.description:
The Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF (NYSEARCA:AVDV) is an exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to non-U.S. developed small-cap companies. The strategy takes a value approach to constructing the portfolio.

About Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF

AVDV was launched on Sept. 24, 2019, with American Century Investment Management acting as the investment advisor. AVDV is an actively managed strategy, meaning the portfolio management team actively engages in security selection and security weightings within the strategy. The objective of the strategy is to achieve long-term capital appreciation through investment in small-cap international value companies. AVDV benchmarks to the MSCI World Ex-U.S. Small Cap Index.

AVDV has $11.76 billion in assets under management [AUM] with an average daily trading volume of $50 million in share value, creating substantial depth for investors managing a position. The strategy provides adequate liquidity with a low spread risk of 6bps, well below the ETF median of 13bps. AVDV also pays out a semiannual distribution at an annualized rate of $3.07/share, yielding 3.57% on a trailing 12-month basis. The portfolio strategy has exhibited consistent growth over the years, providing substantial value to long-term investors.

The strategy identifies companies based on market cap and a number of fundamental factors, including book value, operating cash flow, and accruals, valuing companies based on adjusted book value/price. As part of the investment initiative, AVDV may invest in common stock, preferreds, equity-equivalent securities like convertibles, and derivatives like futures contracts. As an actively managed strategy, the fund may take a temporary defensive approach during adverse market conditions.

In total, AVDV has 1,413 holdings that make up the strategy, with the top 10 holdings making up 7.77% of the total portfolio weight. No single security makes up more than 1% of the total portfolio weight, suggesting substantial diversification across all holdings. The strategy is well diversified across regions; Japan exhibits the largest country concentration, making up 32% of the total portfolio weight, followed by the UK at 12%, and Canada at 10%.

The strategy is also well diversified across industries, with industrials & materials making up the largest proportion of the portfolio with sector weights of 24% & 19%, respectively.

The top holding in the strategy is Mitsui Mining and Smelting Co. (OTCPK:MMSMY), making up 0.97% of the total portfolio weight. Mitsui Mining + Smelting Co. is a Japan-based materials producer, offering various mining materials, battery materials, and fabricated metal materials, among other products.

The second-largest holding is Marks and Spencer Group (OTCQX:MAKSF), accounting for 0.94% of the total portfolio weight. Marks and Spencer Group is a UK-based retailer, operating outlets for fashion, home & beauty, and food, among others.

Looking at peer strategies, AVDV is one of the lowest cost strategies, with an expense ratio of 36bps, well below most ETF peers. AVDV has generally outperformed peers on a total return basis when compared over most historical periods. One strategy that has performed closely to AVDV is the First Trust Developed Markets Ex-U.S. Small Cap AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FDTS), which has $8.23 million in AUM and an expense ratio of 80bps. Despite the comparable performance, FDTS is significantly smaller and may not provide the level of liquidity required by larger investment advisors for allocation.

In general, small-cap stocks outperform during periods of easing interest rate policy set by central banks. Lower rates can be beneficial to small-cap companies given that debt may be relatively more expensive when compared to issuances by large-cap companies. Interest payments by small-cap companies may also be relatively more impactful to the bottom line relative to large-cap companies. With rate policy easing, financing costs may become more appealing for small-cap companies. So far in 2025, the European Central Bank [ ECB] has cut the key rate from 2.75% to 2%. Looking ahead, the ECB may hold rates steady for the time being following the EU-U.S. trade deal agreement. In turn, the US Federal Reserve is expected to cut the key rate in the upcoming September FOMC meeting; this may potentially lead to the euro becoming more expensive relative to the U.S. Dollar, creating a more appealing market environment for European companies doing business in the United States.

On the other hand, Japan has been in a rate hike cycle since 2024. The country may continue increasing the key interest rate following multiple years of negative interest rates. Despite the increasing rate environment, I do not believe the current rate environment can be considered as restrictive.

An interesting factor to consider is the $1.15 trillion U.S. investment outlay between the EU and Japan as a result of the recent trade agreements. This could potentially bolster foreign companies’ businesses that seek to have a presence in the US. If the US Federal Reserve were to cut the key rate, foreign companies may realize an appealing foreign exchange environment, bolstering cash flows as a result.

Risks Related to AVDV

AVDV is an actively managed international portfolio strategy; given the construction of the strategy, there are several risks an investor must consider before making an investment decision. Investors in AVDV are dependent on the skills of the portfolio management team, resulting in management risk. The strategy also invests in foreign equities, potentially exposing investors to interest rate risk, inflation risk, foreign exchange risk, international trade risk, geopolitical risk, and liquidity risk, among other risks. Interest rate policy across regions can impact equities to a certain extent, and regional rate policy may vary. Rate policy can impact foreign exchange rates and may potentially impact company cash flows. Trade policies and tariffs may also influence regional macroeconomics, potentially resulting in inflationary pressure and shifts in the supply chains.

Final Thoughts

AVDV is an appealing investment strategy, providing investors with international small-cap exposure across value-oriented equity investments. AVDV has historically outperformed peer strategies and may continue to outperform given the portfolio management team’s investment strategy, though investors should be aware that past performance is not suggestive of future returns. Given the risks and portfolio construction, I am recommending AVDV with a "Buy" rating with a target allocation of 3%-6% for international equity exposure.

data.title: August ADP Employment Points To Slower Hiring Pace Ahead Of Jobs Report
data.description:
The latest ADP employment report points to a labor market that continues to cool but remains uneven beneath the surface. Hiring momentum has slowed from earlier in the summer, with notable divergences between industries and firm sizes, while wage growth shows only gradual signs of easing. Together with a rise in announced job cuts, the data underscores a labor market losing some of its heat, setting the stage for tomorrow’s official jobs report to carry even more weight for markets and policymakers.

ADP Employment

The ADP employment data released today gives us a preview of what private payroll growth might look like tomorrow in the BLS employment situation report. The headline private payroll employment gain, based on ADP’s data, is just 54,000 for August, slower than the 106,000 added in July (revised up from the initial report of 104,000) and below expectations of a gain of 65,000. However, August job gains did outpace the weaker May and June readings, which resulted in an increase in the 3-month trend from 37,000 in July to 46,000. So while the number does look soft compared to the forecast and the July gain, August hiring appears to have been more solid than what we saw at the end of Q2.

Job growth was seen in both the goods and services sectors, with some divergence across industries.

  • The private goods sector added 13,000 jobs, the weakest since the sector saw employment decline in May. Within the sector, an increase of 16,000 construction jobs led the way, while natural resources & mining added just 4,000, and manufacturing was down -7,000. It is notable that the construction industry saw another decent month of job gains (+15,000 in July) since it is known as a cyclically sensitive segment of the economy.
  • The private services sector added 42,000 jobs, continuing to be the primary source of employment growth in the private sector. However, many service industries reported a weak month, and the overall service sector increase in payrolls was driven by one strong industry. That industry was leisure & hospitality, which saw an increase of 50,000 jobs in August, building on strength that it saw earlier in the summer (July: +46,000, June: +32,000). Outside of that industry, smaller gains in information (+7,000) and professional services (+15,000) were offset by declines in trade, transport & utilities (-17,000), education & health (-12,000), and financial activities (-2,000).

Another positive takeaway from the data release this morning is that the growth in private employment was seen across all firm sizes. Mid-sized firms saw the strongest growth at 25,000, but large and small firms were not far behind with gains of 18,000 and 12,000, respectively. Even though it was the weakest, the growth in small business employment is notable because, like construction employment, it is cyclically sensitive. Thus, we don’t have to worry about a leading signal flashing red. Though it is worth noting that small business employment is actually slightly down over the last month since it dropped 47,000 in June, offsetting the 24,000 small business jobs added in August and July.

ADP Wage Growth

The other component of the ADP National Employment Report is ADP’s measures of pay growth. Over the last two years, the measures have been mostly stable and very slightly decreasing. The August data did not show a divergence. Annual pay growth for “job changers” remained unchanged at 7.1%, remaining stubbornly high so far this year. Annual pay growth for “job stayers” ticked down to 4.4%, another signal of a slow deceleration in compensation. It is worth noting that ADP’s pay growth measures are very new, with data only going back to 2020. So it’s hard to tell how these readings compared to pre-pandemic periods. If there is anything to take away from this data, it’s that in the near term, there haven’t been any major changes in employee compensation, which is consistent with the stickiness we have seen in the BLS’s average hourly earnings data.

Job Cuts

Before the ADP data was released, Challenger, Gray & Christmas released their report on job cuts announced in the US. The report showed job cuts up 39% MoM and 13% YoY to 85,979 in August, further evidence that the labor market is cooling. So far this year, US companies have announced 892,362 job cuts, the highest for a Jan-Aug period since 2020. This total includes cuts announced by the federal government, labeled as “DOGE” cuts, which have totaled 292,279 so far this year. However, besides that, macro conditions are a major reason causing employers to make cuts to their workforces. “Market and economic conditions” have been the 2nd most popular reason for cutting at 199,267, followed by “store closings” at 131,030 and “restructuring” at 96,871.

The job cuts data point is not one of the major data points used to assess the labor market since it doesn’t describe a confirmed layoff, but it is still a measure of sentiment in hiring worth keeping in mind. The August job cuts report is just another piece of evidence that joins a chorus of information that is pointing to a weakening labor market.

Market Reaction

With the ADP employment report being more of a second-tier source of employment data (if BLS is considered first-tier), the reaction in markets was not as clear-cut. In general, it looks like traders who did pay attention perceived the report as a solid reading of job gains despite the headline number coming in slightly below expectations.

  • S&P futures appear to have made a slight move lower in the immediate aftermath of the report, but from peak to trough, the decline was only about -0.1% over the course of the hour following the release. At market open, stocks traded higher with the S&P 500 up 0.2% and NASDAQ roughly flat. Overall, the ADP data was not decisive enough to create a significant move in equities, especially since volatility will be expected tomorrow.
  • The bond market did not see any convincing moves either. In the 30 minutes after the release of the ADP report, yields appeared to tick higher, but the overall trend this morning of a lower yield curve is still intact. At the open of the NY trading session, the 2-year Treasury yield was down around -2 bps, and the 10-year Treasury yield was down around -3 bps. Again, investors did not see decisiveness in the ADP data and will be expecting volatility tomorrow.

Jobs Report Preview

All eyes will be on the August jobs report tomorrow since it will be the last major labor market report before the Fed meets this month. Forecasters believe that job gains in August will be similar to what they were in July, with a consensus estimate of 75,000 for nonfarm payroll employment gains, which would be the strongest reading of the summer, pulling up the 3-month trend readings of both headline and private payroll growth, which were at 35,000 and 52,000 in July, respectively. If we were to see solid job gains in July and August (which would require that the July number does not see any nasty downward revisions), it might be enough to recontextualize the May and June weakness as a temporary blip that can be dismissed.

While the Establishment data is expected to remain solid, analysts expect the employment rate to tick up to 4.3% in August from the July reading of 4.2%. This seems like it would be a significant jump, but more precise data shows that the July unemployment rate is actually around 4.248%, so the August rate would only have to be 0.2 bps higher to round to 4.3%. In fact, there is a good chance the unemployment rate surprises to the upside since a 1.2 bps rise in the unemployment rate would take the August jobless rate to 4.4%. Ignoring the intricacies of the data, any rise in the unemployment rate at all would bring it to another almost three-year high, above the readings last summer when the Fed decided to cut by 50 bps.

With all this in mind, here are my scenarios for tomorrow’s jobs report:

August Jobs Report Scenarios Job gains: (< 70,000) Job gains ( ≥70,000, ≤ 100,000) Job gains (≥ 100,000)
Unemployment rate (< 4.2%) Choppy market that ends hawkish due to tight labor supply. Hawkish markets with yields rising and stocks slightly down, labor market seen as in balance. Very hawkish move, sharp rise in yields, stocks down, Fed pause probability close to 50% as labor market is much stronger than expected.
Unemployment rate (= 4..2% or 4.3%) Neutral markets with yields slightly lower to flat, stocks flat to slightly higher, focus on labor demand easing slightly Neutral markets with yields flat and stocks flat or slightly lower, focus on solid labor demand. Hawkish markets, yields higher, stocks down, Fed pause probability up to 30-40%
Unemployment rate (≥ 4.4%) Dovish markets with yields down significantly and stocks up. November cut probability up significantly. Dovish market with yields down and stocks flat or slightly up, focus on looser labor supply. Choppy market that ends dovish due to loose labor supply.

data.title: AeroVironment Earnings Preview: Ready For A Defense Supercycle Rerating
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on AeroVironment, Inc. (NASDAQ:AVAV) with a target price of $300 (+25% upside from now). The company is at the heart of the new defense supercycle, producing drones, loitering munitions, and anti-drone systems. With the acquisition of BlueHalo, its TAM has multiplied by 5, opening up a huge market. Government interest and military spending are on the rise, and in the following paragraphs, I will explain why I believe this positioning justifies a higher multiple than in the past.

Company Overview

Once known as the "drone company," it has now expanded its fleet, becoming a veritable defense technology platform covering air, land, sea, space, and cyber. The business is divided into three main segments:

  1. Uncrewed Systems (UxS), i.e., reconnaissance drones such as Puma and Jump-20;
  2. Loitering Munition Systems (LMS), which includes Switchblade and Red Dragon loitering munitions;
  3. MacCready Works (MW), the laboratory that develops advanced solutions in directed energy, space, and cyber.

It has successfully developed its business in recent months, delivering the first 20kW AMP-HEL laser systems mounted on U.S. Army light vehicles, thus marking its operational entry into the direct energy C-UAS segment.

AeroVironment has also announced a partnership with Sierra Nevada Corporation (SNC) to develop the Golden Dome for America project (a layered defense architecture based on the principles of Israel's Iron Dome, but adapted to protect U.S. territory). In terms of innovations, it recently unveiled Skyfall for Mars exploration, which builds on the experience gained with NASA's Ingenuity helicopter.

On the industrial front, the acquisition of BlueHalo (EV approximately $4.1 billion) expanded the overall TAM to over $50 billion, bringing in counter-UAS, cyber, and space capabilities that increase production scalability and relevance in Department of Defense programs. Over the past five years, AeroVironment's share of Department of Defense UAS contracts has grown from 2% to 7%, a sign that the company is gaining ground in a highly selective sector, with a record backlog of over $700 million and annual orders of $1.2 billion (just to be clear, these are numbers that give a mid-cap company truly rare visibility).

The next quarterly report is scheduled for September 9, and attention will be focused not only on the company's results but also on the trajectory of orders and the speed with which BlueHalo will be integrated. If these points are confirmed, the stock has room for a structural rerating.

Earnings Preview – September 9

As I mentioned earlier, we are approaching the publication of the company's results. I won't dwell too much on previous results because they are now far behind us, but it is worth remembering that the company closed the previous quarter with revenues of $275.1 million (a 40% increase year over year), adjusted EPS of $1.61 (compared to $0.43 in the previous year) and adjusted EBITDA of $61.6 million, almost triple the previous figure. Even more significant is the order data, because annual bookings were $1.2 billion (an 82% increase year over year) and the financed backlog rose to $726.6 million, the highest level ever recorded. Notably, the stock rose more than 20% immediately after the results.

Attention now shifts to September 9, when the FY26 first quarter results will be released, which will also be the first financial statements to fully reflect the integration of BlueHalo. Rather than focusing on the absolute figures, everyone's attention should be focused on the pace of order growth and the trajectory of margins. The guidance provided by management in the latest quarterly report indicates revenues between $1.9 and $2.0 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $300-$320 million, and adjusted EPS between $2.80 and $3.00 for the entire FY26. With a record backlog of over $700 million and annual bookings of $1.2 billion, the September quarter will show whether the company can turn this visibility into consistent execution.

There are two aspects that I will personally be following closely.

  1. The first is revenue: I want to understand how much the higher-margin segments, such as loitering munitions and anti-drone systems, will grow compared to the more “legacy” part of reconnaissance drones.
  2. The second point I will be following closely concerns BlueHalo, as I mentioned earlier. I want to understand whether the larger industrial scale is already translating into synergies in production and SG&A costs. Here, AeroVironment will have to demonstrate that it has not simply bought growth, but is building real efficiency.

The current context is also favorable for the company. Suffice it to say that for fiscal year 2026, the Department of Defense has already allocated a substantial budget for drones and anti-drone systems, while in Europe, NATO is racing to build up its stockpiles. If there is further positive news regarding the order book (e.g., new contracts in the AMP-HEL programs or long-range reconnaissance systems), then the stock could benefit from this new catalyst.

Valuation

Before evaluating AeroVironment, we must remember two things:

  1. how much the revenue base can grow after the integration of BlueHalo.
  2. what multiple justifies a company that today operates as a “mini-prime” in defense, being a mid-cap overall.

As mentioned earlier, guidance for FY26 forecasts revenues of between $1.9 and $2.0 billion, with adjusted EBITDA of $300–320 million and adjusted EPS of $2.80–3.00. For those who read my analyses, I try to do the math step by step, so as not to get lost and to be as transparent as possible. I use the EV/EBITDA CY26E method. Let's do the math:

  • Let's take the middle of the guidance, so EBITDA of $310 million.
  • I apply a premium multiple compared to peers (defense-tech sector ~21×, AVAV already trades at >30× today). Here I make my considerations and take into account above-average growth. In fact, AVA is one of the few defense stocks capable of growing at tech rates, despite being an “industrial” stock. It also has unique exposure to the military defense budget (US and Europe), and finally, I take into account the acquisition of BlueHalo. Given these premises, I feel I can prudently and without optimism choose a multiple of 40×.
  • EV = $310M × 40 = $12.4 billion.
  • Following the capital increase and the issue of 0% 2030 convertible bonds, the company stated that it had used most of the proceeds to reduce debt and strengthen its cash position.
